source_text: I used to walk to school every morning before I moved to another city.

reference_translations.text: Andavo a scuola a piedi ogni mattina prima di trasferirmi in un'altra città.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto for the habitual past walking, infinitive 'di trasferirmi' after 'prima di'.

reference_translations.text: Ogni mattina andavo a scuola a piedi prima di cambiare città.
reference_translations.notes: Adverb fronted, slightly more natural rhythm; 'cambiare città' is a casual alternative for 'trasferirmi'.

reference_translations.text: Sono andato a scuola a piedi ogni mattina prima di trasferirmi in un'altra città.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: passato prossimo with 'ogni mattina' fails to capture the habitual reading. Anchor against the default-to-PP miss for English 'used to'.

translation.reveal: to walk - camminare / andare a piedi

explanation: When you describe what you used to do daily as part of a past routine, the verb names a habit rather than a single trip. Italian uses the imperfect indicative for habitual past actions. 'Ogni mattina' and 'used to' both flag the habit. Sono andato would commit the walking to a single past day, which clashes with the recurring frame.

source_text: Yesterday morning I bumped into Marco at the supermarket while I was doing the shopping.

reference_translations.text: Ieri mattina ho incontrato Marco al supermercato mentre facevo la spesa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP for the punctual event 'ho incontrato', imperfetto for the background 'facevo la spesa'.

reference_translations.text: Ieri mattina, mentre facevo la spesa, ho incontrato Marco al supermercato.
reference_translations.notes: Background clause fronted; common Italian word-order choice for narrative effect.

translation.reveal: to bump into / run into - incontrare (informally)
to do the shopping - fare la spesa

explanation: When a past event drops into an unfolding scene, Italian splits the tenses: passato prossimo for the event and imperfetto for the scene. The single bump-into is the event; the doing-the-shopping is the backdrop. The conjunction 'mentre' is a textbook cue for the imperfetto on the verb it governs.

source_text: While I was reading the newspaper, the doorbell rang twice in a row.

reference_translations.text: Mentre leggevo il giornale, il campanello ha suonato due volte di fila.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto for the reading backdrop, PP for the two distinct rings.

reference_translations.text: Stavo leggendo il giornale quando il campanello ha suonato due volte di fila.
reference_translations.notes: Past progressive 'stavo leggendo' is acceptable here for the vivid reading; the imperfetto remains the more standard choice.

explanation: When a punctual past event interrupts an ongoing past activity, Italian uses the passato prossimo for the event ('ha suonato') and the imperfetto for the activity ('leggevo'). The conjunction 'mentre' marks the reading as the unfolding scene the ring lands inside.

source_text: Ogni domenica andavamo dai nonni a pranzo: era una tradizione di famiglia.

reference_translations.text: Every Sunday we used to go to our grandparents' for lunch: it was a family tradition.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. English 'used to' maps the habitual imperfetto cleanly.

reference_translations.text: We would go to our grandparents' for lunch every Sunday; it was a family tradition.
reference_translations.notes: Habitual 'would' is an alternative English mapping of the imperfetto.

explanation: When the source describes a recurring past family pattern, English needs a tense that signals habit. The imperfetto 'andavamo' maps to 'used to go' or to habitual 'would go'; both renderings preserve the recurring-routine reading. A simple past 'went' would also work in English but loses some of the habitual emphasis.

source_text: Marco è arrivato in ritardo a causa del traffico in centro questa mattina.

reference_translations.text: Marco arrived late this morning because of the traffic in the centre.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Single past event maps to English simple past.

reference_translations.text: Marco was late this morning because of the traffic in town.
reference_translations.notes: Common alternative phrasing with 'was late'.

explanation: When a single completed past event is reported, Italian uses the passato prossimo and English uses the simple past. The definite time anchor 'questa mattina' fixes the lateness to one identifiable moment.

source_text: Erano le sei di sera quando il telefono ha squillato all'improvviso.

reference_translations.text: It was six in the evening when the phone suddenly rang.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'erano' (telling time in past) becomes 'it was'; PP becomes simple past.

reference_translations.text: It was six pm when the phone rang out of the blue.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic 'out of the blue' for 'all'improvviso'.

explanation: When the sentence sets a past time as a backdrop ('erano le sei') and then names a punctual event landing inside it ('ha squillato'), Italian uses the imperfetto for the backdrop and the PP for the event. English maps both to past forms, with 'was' for the time and the simple past for the ring.

source_text: I found out about the wedding only this morning, from a friend.

reference_translations.text: Ho saputo del matrimonio solo stamattina, da un amico.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Ho saputo' carries the event-of-learning reading; 'solo stamattina' pins the moment.

reference_translations.text: Ho saputo del matrimonio solo questa mattina, da un'amica.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with feminine 'amica'; 'questa mattina' is more formal than 'stamattina'.

reference_translations.text: Sapevo del matrimonio solo stamattina, da un amico.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the imperfetto 'sapevo' carries the state-of-knowledge reading, which clashes with 'solo stamattina' (only this morning). Anchor against the default-to-imperfetto miss.

explanation: When you name the moment you found something out, the verb captures an event of learning rather than a continuing state. Italian's modal verb sapere takes the passato prossimo for that event reading. 'Solo stamattina' (only this morning) pins the discovery to one defined point.

source_text: Non sapevo della tua malattia: mi dispiace molto, davvero.

reference_translations.text: I didn't know about your illness: I'm so sorry, really.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'sapevo' maps to English 'didn't know' for the state of ignorance.

reference_translations.text: I had no idea about your illness: I'm really sorry.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic 'I had no idea' captures the state-of-not-knowing nuance.

explanation: When the source describes a continuing state of not knowing something up to a moment of disclosure, English uses 'didn't know' (or the idiom 'had no idea') rather than 'didn't find out'. Italian's modal verb sapere uses the imperfetto for that state-of-knowledge reading even under negation.

source_text: Ho saputo della loro separazione dalla radio mentre tornavo a casa.

reference_translations.text: I found out about their separation from the radio as I was driving home.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Ho saputo' maps to 'found out'; imperfetto 'tornavo' maps to 'was driving home'.

reference_translations.text: I heard about their separation on the radio while I was on my way home.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic 'heard about' for 'ho saputo' in the event-of-learning sense.

explanation: When the source names the moment of learning a piece of news, English uses 'found out' or 'heard about' rather than 'knew'. Italian's modal verb sapere in the PP carries the event-of-learning reading; the imperfetto would describe a prior state of knowing, which doesn't fit the source's framing of fresh discovery.

source_text: I had to work all night to finish the report on time.

reference_translations.text: Ho dovuto lavorare tutta la notte per finire la relazione in tempo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP 'ho dovuto' carries the had-to-and-did reading; 'relazione' is the standard term for a work report.

reference_translations.text: Ho dovuto lavorare fino al mattino per consegnare il rapporto in tempo.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'fino al mattino' for 'all night'; 'rapporto' is interchangeable with 'relazione'.

explanation: When an obligation was met and the action carried out, Italian's modal verb dovere uses the passato prossimo for the 'had to and did' reading. The result clause 'to finish the report on time' implies the work happened.

source_text: She was supposed to call me at six, but the phone never rang that evening.

reference_translations.text: Doveva chiamarmi alle sei, ma il telefono non è mai squillato quella sera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'doveva' for the 'was supposed to' reading; the contradiction in the second clause confirms the obligation was unfulfilled.

reference_translations.text: Mi doveva chiamare alle sei, ma quella sera il telefono non ha squillato.
reference_translations.notes: Clitic fronting; common variation in spoken Italian.

reference_translations.text: Ha dovuto chiamarmi alle sei, ma il telefono non è mai squillato quella sera.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the PP 'ha dovuto' means she had to and did, which contradicts the phone never ringing. Anchor against the default-to-PP miss.

explanation: When the source flags an obligation that was on the books but didn't get fulfilled, Italian's modal verb dovere uses the imperfetto for the 'was supposed to' reading. The 'but...never rang' clause makes the unfulfilled reading explicit.

source_text: Ieri ho dovuto rimanere in ufficio fino a mezzanotte per una scadenza importante.

reference_translations.text: Yesterday I had to stay at the office until midnight because of an important deadline.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Ho dovuto' (PP) maps to English 'had to' with the implied 'and did'.

reference_translations.text: I had to stay in the office until midnight yesterday for an important deadline.
reference_translations.notes: Time adverb repositioned; equivalent meaning.

explanation: When the source describes a past obligation that was carried out, English uses 'had to' with implied completion. Italian's modal verb dovere in the PP carries the 'had to and did' reading; the time anchor 'ieri...fino a mezzanotte' confirms the work happened.

source_text: I could have helped you yesterday, but you didn't ask me.

reference_translations.text: Potevo aiutarti ieri, ma non me l'hai chiesto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'potevo' for the 'could have' reading; the contradiction confirms the help wasn't given.

reference_translations.text: Ti avrei potuto aiutare ieri, ma non me l'hai chiesto.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with past conditional ('avrei potuto'), more explicit about the 'could have' counterfactual reading.

reference_translations.text: Ho potuto aiutarti ieri, ma non me l'hai chiesto.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the PP 'ho potuto' means 'I managed to help', which contradicts the second clause. Anchor against the default-to-PP miss.

explanation: When the source describes a past ability that went unused, Italian's modal verb potere uses the imperfetto for the 'could have' reading. The clause 'but you didn't ask me' confirms the help wasn't given. The past conditional 'avrei potuto' is a more explicit alternative.

source_text: After many attempts, I finally managed to reach my brother on the phone.

reference_translations.text: Dopo molti tentativi, finalmente ho potuto contattare mio fratello al telefono.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP 'ho potuto' carries the 'managed to' reading; 'finalmente' reinforces the breakthrough.

reference_translations.text: Dopo molti tentativi, alla fine sono riuscito a parlare con mio fratello al telefono.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative: 'sono riuscito a' (managed to) is an even more explicit rendering; both work.

explanation: When the source frames an achievement as the result of repeated effort, Italian's modal verb potere uses the passato prossimo for the 'managed to' reading. 'Dopo molti tentativi' and 'finalmente' both flag the success as a breakthrough event.

source_text: Da giovane potevo studiare per ore senza stancarmi minimamente.

reference_translations.text: When I was young, I could study for hours without getting the least bit tired.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'potevo' maps to habitual past 'could'.

reference_translations.text: As a young person, I was able to study for hours without tiring at all.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'was able to'; 'da giovane' becomes 'as a young person'.

explanation: When the source describes a sustained past ability tied to a life-stage, English maps the imperfetto 'potevo' to 'could' or 'was able to' in a habitual past reading. The frame 'da giovane' (when I was young) marks the ability as something that ran across a long period.

source_text: She wanted to tell me the truth, but she didn't have the courage that evening.

reference_translations.text: Voleva dirmi la verità, ma non ha avuto il coraggio quella sera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'voleva' for the unfulfilled wish; the contradiction confirms it didn't happen.

reference_translations.text: Voleva dirmi la verità, ma quella sera non ne ha avuto il coraggio.
reference_translations.notes: Adverb fronted; uses partitive 'ne' for the courage-of-it referent.

reference_translations.text: Ha voluto dirmi la verità, ma non ha avuto il coraggio quella sera.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the PP 'ha voluto' means she insisted on telling and did, which contradicts the courage failure. Anchor against the default-to-PP miss.

explanation: When a past wish stayed unfulfilled, Italian's modal verb volere uses the imperfetto for the 'wanted to' reading. The clause 'but she didn't have the courage' confirms the truth-telling didn't happen.

source_text: Ho voluto pagare il conto io, anche se i miei amici avevano insistito per dividere.

reference_translations.text: I insisted on paying the bill myself, even though my friends had insisted on splitting it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Ho voluto' (PP) maps to 'I insisted on'; the trapassato 'avevano insistito' maps to 'had insisted on'.

reference_translations.text: I wanted to pay the bill on my own (and I did), even though my friends had insisted we split it.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative making the insistence reading explicit with 'and I did'.

explanation: When the source describes someone getting their way against the protests of others, English maps 'ho voluto' to 'insisted on' rather than 'wanted to'. Italian's modal verb volere in the PP carries the insistence reading; the imperfetto would only describe a wish.

source_text: Volevo dirti una cosa importante, ma poi mi è passato di mente.

reference_translations.text: I wanted to tell you something important, but then it slipped my mind.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'volevo' maps to English 'wanted to' for the unfulfilled intention.

reference_translations.text: I meant to tell you something important, but then I forgot.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'meant to', which captures the unfulfilled-intention nuance cleanly.

translation.reveal: passare di mente - to slip one's mind

explanation: When the source describes an intention that the speaker didn't act on (because they forgot), English uses 'wanted to' or the idiomatic 'meant to'. Italian's modal verb volere in the imperfetto carries that unfulfilled-wish reading; the PP would suggest the action was actually carried out.

source_text: I met my husband at a friend's birthday party twenty years ago.

reference_translations.text: Ho conosciuto mio marito a una festa di compleanno di un amico vent'anni fa.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP 'ho conosciuto' carries the event-of-meeting reading.

reference_translations.text: Ho conosciuto mio marito alla festa di compleanno di un'amica, vent'anni fa.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with feminine 'amica' and articulated preposition 'alla'.

reference_translations.text: Conoscevo mio marito a una festa di compleanno di un amico vent'anni fa.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the imperfetto 'conoscevo' means 'I was already acquainted with', which contradicts the first-meeting reading. Anchor against the default-to-imperfetto miss.

explanation: When the source describes the first time of meeting someone, Italian's modal verb conoscere uses the passato prossimo for the event-of-meeting reading. The setting cues 'a una festa di compleanno...vent'anni fa' fix the meeting as a defined past moment.

source_text: Conoscevo Anna già da molto tempo prima del matrimonio: eravamo cresciute insieme.

reference_translations.text: I had known Anna for a long time before the wedding: we had grown up together.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'conoscevo' with 'da molto tempo' maps to English past perfect 'had known'.

reference_translations.text: I'd known Anna for ages before the wedding; we'd grown up together.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic 'for ages' captures the long-standing duration.

explanation: When the source describes a long-standing acquaintance up to a past moment, English uses 'had known' (past perfect) rather than 'met'. Italian's modal verb conoscere in the imperfetto carries the state-of-acquaintance reading; the duration phrase 'da molto tempo' fixes how far back it stretched.

source_text: Ieri sera, alla cena dei colleghi, abbiamo conosciuto una coppia molto interessante.

reference_translations.text: Last night, at the colleagues' dinner, we met a very interesting couple.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP 'abbiamo conosciuto' maps to English 'met' (first time).

reference_translations.text: Yesterday evening, at the work dinner, we met a really interesting couple for the first time.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'for the first time' making the meeting reading explicit.

explanation: When the source describes meeting new people at a specific event, English uses 'met' (simple past) rather than 'knew'. Italian's modal verb conoscere in the PP carries the event-of-meeting reading; the cues 'ieri sera...alla cena' pin the introduction to a defined moment.

source_text: Carlo has been working at the bank for fifteen years now.

reference_translations.text: Carlo lavora in banca da quindici anni ormai.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's simple present + 'da' + duration maps English present perfect continuous.

reference_translations.text: Carlo è in banca da quindici anni ormai.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative omitting 'lavora' and using 'essere in banca' (works at the bank); more conversational.

reference_translations.text: Carlo sta lavorando in banca da quindici anni ormai.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: Italian's present progressive isn't used for a sustained occupation; the construction 'sta lavorando da quindici anni' grates against native usage. Anchor against the carry-over-from-English-progressive miss.

explanation: When English uses present perfect continuous to describe a long-standing occupation, Italian uses simple present + 'da' + duration. The simple present 'lavora' marks the working as a stable state, and 'da quindici anni' anchors the duration. The Italian present progressive would clash with the long-term reading.

source_text: I can't talk now, I'm preparing an important presentation for tomorrow.

reference_translations.text: Non posso parlare adesso, sto preparando una presentazione importante per domani.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Present progressive 'sto preparando' for the genuinely-in-progress activity.

reference_translations.text: Non posso parlarti ora, sto preparando una presentazione importante per domani.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with attached clitic 'parlarti' and 'ora' instead of 'adesso'.

explanation: When the action is genuinely unfolding right now and the speaker is flagging an interruption, Italian's present progressive earns its keep. The cues 'non posso parlare adesso' fix the call to the live moment. The simple present 'preparo' would describe a habit, losing the right-now emphasis.

source_text: Mia sorella studia legge all'università di Bologna da tre anni.

reference_translations.text: My sister has been studying law at the University of Bologna for three years.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian simple present 'studia...da' maps to English present perfect continuous.

reference_translations.text: My sister has been studying law in Bologna for three years.
reference_translations.notes: Less verbose alternative.

explanation: When the Italian uses simple present + 'da' + duration to describe an ongoing study programme, English needs present perfect continuous to capture the 'started in the past, still going' reading. A bare English simple present 'My sister studies law' would lose the duration; English simple past 'studied' would suggest she's finished.

source_text: While I was making dinner, the phone rang twice in quick succession.

reference_translations.text: Mentre preparavo la cena, il telefono ha squillato due volte in rapida successione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'preparavo' for the past ongoing action; PP 'ha squillato' for the punctual events.

reference_translations.text: Il telefono ha squillato due volte di seguito mentre stavo preparando la cena.
reference_translations.notes: Past progressive 'stavo preparando' is acceptable but slightly marked; the imperfetto remains the unmarked choice.

explanation: When English uses 'was -ing' for a past ongoing action, Italian's default is the imperfetto, not the past progressive. The past progressive 'stavo preparando' is reserved for vivid emphasis; in this neutral narrative the imperfetto fits better. The conjunction 'mentre' is a textbook cue for the imperfetto on the verb it governs.

source_text: Stavo proprio pensando a te quando mi hai chiamato, che coincidenza!

reference_translations.text: I was just thinking about you when you called, what a coincidence!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's past progressive 'stavo pensando' maps to English's past progressive 'was thinking'.

reference_translations.text: I was thinking about you at that very moment when you called, what a coincidence!
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'at that very moment' making the vivid coincidence explicit.

explanation: When the Italian uses the past progressive 'stavo pensando' for a vivid in-the-moment action that coincides with another event, English uses 'was thinking' for the same effect. Both languages reach for the progressive form here; the marked construction carries the 'right at that very moment' emphasis the bare imperfetto wouldn't capture.

source_text: She must be about sixty years old, judging by her appearance.

reference_translations.text: Avrà una sessantina d'anni, a giudicare dall'aspetto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Future of probability 'avrà' renders English's 'must be' for guessing.

reference_translations.text: Deve avere circa sessant'anni, a giudicare dall'aspetto.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with modal of probability 'deve avere'; both are widely used.

explanation: When English uses 'must be' to mark a guess about a present fact, Italian reaches for the morphological future ('avrà') to mark the same uncertainty. The cue 'judging by her appearance' confirms the speaker is estimating rather than reporting.

source_text: One day, when I have more free time, I will write a novel about my grandmother.

reference_translations.text: Un giorno, quando avrò più tempo libero, scriverò un romanzo su mia nonna.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Morphological future for both the conditional 'quando avrò' and the main 'scriverò'.

reference_translations.text: Un giorno, quando avrò del tempo libero in più, scriverò un romanzo su mia nonna.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'del tempo libero in più' for 'more free time'.

explanation: When the source frames a distant, uncertain future plan, Italian uses the morphological future. The cue 'one day...when I have more free time' is open-ended, with no near anchor; the present-for-future shortcut wouldn't fit. Italian also uses the future in the temporal clause introduced by 'quando' here, unlike English which uses simple present.

source_text: Saranno le tre passate ormai, dobbiamo davvero andare via prima del traffico.

reference_translations.text: It must be past three by now, we really need to leave before the traffic.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's future of probability 'saranno' maps to English 'must be'.

reference_translations.text: It must be after three o'clock by now, we really should go before the traffic builds up.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'after three o'clock'; both renderings preserve the guess.

explanation: When the Italian uses the morphological future 'saranno' to estimate the present time, English uses 'must be' to mark the same hedging. Both languages avoid asserting the time as known when the speaker is in fact guessing.

source_text: I'm leaving for Rome tomorrow on the eight o'clock train.

reference_translations.text: Parto domani per Roma con il treno delle otto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's simple present 'parto' for an anchored near-future plan.

reference_translations.text: Domani parto per Roma con il treno delle otto.
reference_translations.notes: Time adverb fronted; equivalent meaning.

explanation: When the source describes a settled near-future plan with a specific time anchor, Italian uses the simple present rather than the morphological future. English's 'I'm leaving tomorrow' uses the present progressive for the same effect; Italian's simple present 'parto' is the standard equivalent.

source_text: Lunedì prossimo vado dal dentista per il controllo annuale.

reference_translations.text: Next Monday I'm going to the dentist for my annual check-up.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's simple present 'vado' maps to English's present progressive 'I'm going' for an arranged plan.

reference_translations.text: I'm seeing the dentist next Monday for my annual check-up.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'seeing'; both English renderings preserve the arranged-plan reading.

explanation: When the Italian uses the simple present for an arranged near-future plan, English commonly uses the present progressive ('I'm going') or a simple present with a future time adverb. Both languages treat the appointment as a settled fact rather than an uncertain projection.

source_text: If I had more free time, I would learn Chinese with great pleasure. (formal writing)

reference_translations.text: Se avessi più tempo libero, imparerei il cinese con grande piacere.
reference_translations.notes: Standard prescriptive: imperfect subjunctive in the protasis, present conditional in the apodosis.

reference_translations.text: Se avessi più tempo libero, studierei il cinese con grande piacere.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'studierei' for 'learn'; same structure.

explanation: When a counterfactual appears in formal writing, Italian pairs the imperfect subjunctive in the if-clause with the present conditional in the result clause. The formal-writing register cue fixes the prescriptive form. The colloquial version 'Se avevo più tempo libero, imparavo il cinese' would feel out of place in writing.

source_text: If Garibaldi had not intervened, the Italian unification would have taken a very different path. (historical essay)

reference_translations.text: Se Garibaldi non fosse intervenuto, l'unificazione italiana avrebbe preso un percorso molto diverso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard prescriptive past counterfactual: pluperfect subjunctive in protasis, past conditional in apodosis.

reference_translations.text: Se Garibaldi non fosse intervenuto, l'unificazione italiana avrebbe seguito un percorso molto diverso.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'avrebbe seguito' for 'would have taken'.

explanation: When a past counterfactual appears in formal essayistic prose, Italian pairs the pluperfect subjunctive ('fosse intervenuto') with the past conditional ('avrebbe preso'). The historical-essay cue locks the prescriptive register; the colloquial imperfetto-imperfetto version would clash with the scholarly tone.

source_text: Se avessi saputo della tua malattia, sarei venuto a trovarti subito. (formal letter)

reference_translations.text: If I had known about your illness, I would have come to see you straight away.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. English past counterfactual: 'had known' + 'would have come'.

reference_translations.text: Had I known about your illness, I would have come to visit you immediately.
reference_translations.notes: Inverted protasis 'Had I known' is more formal; appropriate for a letter.

explanation: When the Italian uses the prescriptive past counterfactual (pluperfect subjunctive + past conditional), English maps to 'had known' + 'would have come'. Both languages share the same formal counterfactual scaffolding for past unrealised possibilities.

source_text: If I'd known earlier, I would have come right away to help. (a chat between close friends)

reference_translations.text: Se sapevo prima, venivo subito ad aiutare.
reference_translations.notes: Colloquial counterfactual: imperfetto in both clauses. Fits the chatty register. Fires the colloquial_imperfect bucket as a hit; prescriptive_conditional stays untouched (asymmetric tracking per Architecture's marker ruling, 2026-05-28).

reference_translations.text: Se l'avessi saputo prima, sarei venuto subito ad aiutare.
reference_translations.notes: Prescriptive form, also acceptable in this informal context. Fires the prescriptive_conditional bucket as a hit; colloquial_imperfect stays untouched. Item credit is full either way; the two buckets are first-class peers.

reference_translations.text: Se sapevo prima, sarei venuto subito ad aiutare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: mixing colloquial protasis (sapevo) with prescriptive apodosis (sarei venuto) is dispreferred. Either keep both colloquial or both prescriptive.

explanation: When a counterfactual appears in casual, friend-to-friend speech, Italian widely uses the imperfetto in both clauses. The informal-chat register cue licenses the colloquial form; the prescriptive form remains technically correct but feels stiff. Either is accepted on this item, with the marker firing whichever register's bucket matches the learner's choice. The two forms should not be mixed within the same sentence.

source_text: Se vincevo la lotteria quella sera, portavo tutti in vacanza in Sicilia, te lo giuro!

reference_translations.text: If I had won the lottery that evening, I would have taken everyone on holiday to Sicily, I swear!
reference_translations.notes: Standard. English needs the formal counterfactual ('had won' + 'would have taken') even when the Italian uses the colloquial imperfetto-imperfetto; English doesn't have a parallel colloquial form.

reference_translations.text: If I'd won the lottery that night, I would have taken everyone on a holiday to Sicily, I swear!
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with contraction 'I'd' and 'night' for 'sera'.

explanation: When the Italian uses the colloquial imperfetto in both clauses for vivid storytelling, English still uses its formal counterfactual ('had won' + 'would have taken'), because English doesn't have a tense alternative that signals colloquial register the same way. The vividness comes through in tone and exclamations rather than tense choice.

source_text: I think Maria is right on this point, even if not everyone agrees with her. (formal writing)

reference_translations.text: Penso che Maria abbia ragione su questo punto, anche se non tutti sono d'accordo con lei.
reference_translations.notes: Standard prescriptive: opinion verb 'penso che' + present subjunctive 'abbia'.

reference_translations.text: Credo che Maria abbia ragione su questo punto, anche se non tutti sono d'accordo con lei.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'credo che' for 'penso che'; same structure.

reference_translations.text: Penso che Maria ha ragione su questo punto, anche se non tutti sono d'accordo con lei.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG for formal writing: the indicative 'ha ragione' is widely heard in speech but feels wrong in writing. Anchor against the colloquial-in-writing miss.

explanation: When an opinion verb like 'penso che' governs a clause in formal writing, Italian uses the present subjunctive in the embedded verb. The formal-writing register cue fixes the prescriptive form. The colloquial indicative 'ha ragione' is heard in speech but dispreferred in writing.

source_text: The report suggests that the government should adopt stricter measures on emissions. (policy paper)

reference_translations.text: Il rapporto suggerisce che il governo debba adottare misure più severe sulle emissioni.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Suggerire che' + present subjunctive 'debba'.

reference_translations.text: Il rapporto suggerisce che il governo adotti misure più severe sulle emissioni.
reference_translations.notes: Variant without modal: 'adotti' directly. Both renderings are common in policy writing.

explanation: When 'suggerire che' governs a clause expressing a recommendation, Italian uses the present subjunctive. The policy-paper register fixes the formal context. The colloquial indicative would clash with the careful tone the document expects.

source_text: Credo che il film sia molto interessante: i critici ne hanno parlato benissimo.

reference_translations.text: I think the film is very interesting: the critics have spoken very highly of it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. English doesn't distinguish indicative from subjunctive in this position; 'is' covers both.

reference_translations.text: I believe the film is very interesting; the critics have praised it highly.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'believe' and 'praised'.

explanation: When the Italian uses the present subjunctive 'sia' after an opinion verb, English maps to the simple indicative 'is'. English lost most of its subjunctive distinctions centuries ago, so the same English form covers what Italian distinguishes via mood.

source_text: I'm glad you are here with me tonight: I really needed your company.

reference_translations.text: Sono contento che tu sia qui con me stasera: avevo proprio bisogno della tua compagnia.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Sono contento che' + present subjunctive 'sia'. Speaker gender masculine here.

reference_translations.text: Sono contenta che tu sia qui con me stasera: avevo proprio bisogno della tua compagnia.
reference_translations.notes: Feminine speaker variant: 'contenta'.

reference_translations.text: Sono felice che tu sia qui con me stasera: avevo proprio bisogno della tua compagnia.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'felice' (gender-invariable, simpler).

explanation: When an emotion expression like 'sono contento che' governs a clause, Italian uses the present subjunctive. The pairing is rigid here, with little colloquial slippage. Even in casual speech, the indicative 'che tu sei qui' feels off.

source_text: Mi dispiace che Carlo non possa venire al matrimonio: ci teneva molto.

reference_translations.text: I'm sorry that Carlo can't come to the wedding: he was really looking forward to it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian subjunctive 'possa' maps to English indicative 'can't'.

reference_translations.text: I'm sorry Carlo can't make it to the wedding; he had really wanted to be there.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'make it' for 'venire' and rephrased second clause.

explanation: When the Italian uses the present subjunctive 'possa' after the emotion trigger 'mi dispiace che', English maps to the simple indicative 'can't'. English no longer marks the subjunctive distinction here, so the same form covers both.

source_text: Although it was very cold that morning, we decided to go to the beach anyway.

reference_translations.text: Sebbene facesse molto freddo quella mattina, abbiamo deciso di andare comunque al mare.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Concessive conjunction 'sebbene' + imperfect subjunctive 'facesse'.

reference_translations.text: Benché facesse molto freddo quel mattino, abbiamo deciso di andare lo stesso al mare.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'benché' (interchangeable with 'sebbene'); 'lo stesso' alternative for 'comunque'.

explanation: When a concessive conjunction ('sebbene', 'benché') introduces a clause, Italian requires the subjunctive in the embedded verb, with almost no colloquial slippage. The conjunction itself is the structural trigger. English 'although' triggers no mood change in the embedded verb.

source_text: Voglio dirti una cosa molto importante prima che tu parta per il viaggio.

reference_translations.text: I want to tell you something very important before you leave for your trip.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian's 'prima che' + subjunctive 'parta' maps to English's 'before' + simple present 'you leave'.

reference_translations.text: I want to say one important thing before you set off on your trip.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'set off' for 'parta'.

explanation: When the Italian uses 'prima che' + subjunctive for an anticipated event, English uses 'before' + simple present. Both languages treat the second clause as something not yet realised, but only Italian marks the verb mood.

source_text: I don't think Anna has arrived yet: her train was late this morning. (formal note)

reference_translations.text: Non penso che Anna sia ancora arrivata: il suo treno era in ritardo stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Negated opinion verb 'non penso che' + past subjunctive 'sia arrivata'.

reference_translations.text: Non credo che Anna sia ancora arrivata: il suo treno aveva ritardo stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'non credo che' and 'aveva ritardo'; same structure.

explanation: When an opinion verb is negated ('non penso che'), Italian keeps the subjunctive in the embedded clause; the subjunctive feels more secure under negation than in the affirmative. The formal-note cue locks the prescriptive register; the indicative 'è ancora arrivata' would feel colloquial.

source_text: When I arrived at the restaurant, the others had already eaten, so I joined them only for dessert.

reference_translations.text: Quando sono arrivato al ristorante, gli altri avevano già mangiato, così mi sono unito a loro solo per il dolce.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP for the arrival, trapassato 'avevano già mangiato' for the prior-completed eating.

reference_translations.text: Quando sono arrivata al ristorante, gli altri avevano già mangiato, perciò mi sono aggiunta a loro solo per il dolce.
reference_translations.notes: Feminine speaker variant with 'aggiunta'; 'perciò' alternative for 'così'.

explanation: When one past event was already finished by the time of another, Italian uses the trapassato prossimo for the earlier action. The adverb 'già' is a textbook cue, and English's 'had already eaten' uses the same past perfect structure. The continuation 'so I joined them only for dessert' confirms the eating was done.

source_text: Era furioso perché qualcuno aveva preso il suo nuovo libro dalla scrivania senza chiedere.

reference_translations.text: He was furious because someone had taken his new book from the desk without asking.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'era furioso' maps to English 'was furious'; trapassato 'aveva preso' maps to past perfect 'had taken'.

reference_translations.text: He was livid because someone had taken his new book off the desk without permission.
reference_translations.notes: Alternative with 'livid' and 'off the desk'.

explanation: When the Italian uses the trapassato prossimo for a completed action that explains a past state, English uses the past perfect for the same structure. Both languages mark the earlier action as finished before the past reference point.

source_text: While Marco was sleeping peacefully on the sofa, the doorbell rang suddenly.

reference_translations.text: Mentre Marco dormiva tranquillamente sul divano, è suonato improvvisamente il campanello.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'dormiva' for the ongoing past background; PP for the interrupting event.

reference_translations.text: Mentre Marco stava dormendo tranquillamente sul divano, il campanello è suonato all'improvviso.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with past progressive 'stava dormendo'; acceptable, slightly more vivid.

explanation: When a past action is still in progress when another event interrupts it, Italian uses the imperfetto for the ongoing action and the PP for the interruption. The conjunction 'mentre' is a textbook cue for the imperfetto. The trapassato would mean the sleeping was already finished, which clashes with the 'in progress when interrupted' reading.

source_text: Pioveva forte quel pomeriggio, così ho deciso di rimanere a casa con un buon libro.

reference_translations.text: It was raining heavily that afternoon, so I decided to stay home with a good book.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfetto 'pioveva' for the past weather state maps to English past progressive 'was raining'.

reference_translations.text: It was pouring that afternoon, so I decided to stay in with a good book.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic 'pouring' for 'pioveva forte' and 'stay in' for 'rimanere a casa'.

explanation: When the Italian uses the imperfetto for past weather as a backdrop to a decision, English uses the past progressive 'was raining' for the same effect. The trapassato 'aveva piovuto' would mean the rain was already over, which wouldn't motivate the decision to stay home.
